questions.title: CBT Session: Opening Joke
questions.scenario: During the homework review regarding 'should' statements, what specific 'bad therapy joke' did the therapist make?

mustInclude: The therapist makes a pun on 'should' sounding like 'sh*t' (i.e., 'should all over yourself')
The joke is phrased as a warning/caution (e.g., 'be careful')
mustAvoid: A joke that does not include the 'should all over yourself' pun
A generic comment about stopping 'shoulds' without the pun
Inventing a different joke (e.g., about traffic signs, perfectionism) not in the transcript

questions.title: CBT Session: Situation Analysis
questions.scenario: In the specific situation discussed (the phone call), at what point in the conversation did the client feel they were 'not ready'?

mustInclude: It happened in the middle of the conversation
It happened when the friend shifted to checking in on her / asking how she was doing / asking about her
mustAvoid: Saying it was at the beginning of the call
Saying it was when he suggested coffee (she was initially okay with that)
Saying it was simply because he called (timing needs the mid-call shift)

questions.title: CBT Session: Metaphor Analysis
questions.scenario: The client introduces a 'stop sign' metaphor regarding the interaction with her friend. According to the transcript, explicitly explain the 'two ways' this stop sign operates.

mustInclude: It stops the client from sharing/telling how she is really doing (e.g., depressed/anxious/'bad')
It stops (or is meant to stop) the friend from asking/checking in (i.e., 'don't ask')
The client believes people aren't ready to hear the truth about how bad she feels
It reflects mutual avoidance/keeping things quiet ("you don't ask and I'm not going to tell")
mustAvoid: Interpreting it as literal traffic rules
Explaining it only as generic boundary-setting without the 'don't ask / don't tell' dynamic
Saying it just means 'stop talking' without specifying both directions

questions.title: CBT Session: Judge Analogy
questions.scenario: When introducing the concept of putting thoughts on trial, how did the therapist describe Judge Judy's personality? List at least two specific descriptors used.

mustInclude: At least two of the following: strong personality; not wishy-washy; not gentle; a little mean; a little gruff
The description is tied to being tough/strict about evidence (not accepting weak evidence)
mustAvoid: Claiming the therapist described her as 'fair' or 'unbiased' (not stated)
Adding unrelated traits not mentioned (e.g., 'loud', 'intelligent') as if they were in the transcript

questions.title: CBT Session: Evidence Ratings
questions.scenario: During the evidence gathering phase, the client and therapist assigned strength ratings (1-3). What rating was assigned to the evidence that 'listening to complaining is annoying', and what rating was assigned to the fact that the friend is a 'support worker'?

mustInclude: Listening to complaining is annoying: rated 3 (client says 'almost like a 3')
Friend being a support worker: rated 3
mustAvoid: Swapping the ratings
Giving non-numeric/unspecified ratings (the question asks for the numbers)
Claiming either was rated 2

questions.title: CBT Session: Evidence Reasoning
questions.scenario: One piece of 'Evidence Against' involved the friend's discretion. How did the client rate the strength of the evidence that he 'hasn't gossiped about her in the past,' and what was the specific reasoning for that low rating?

mustInclude: Rating: 1
Reason: she isn't sure / 'not that I'm aware' / they don't actually know for certain
mustAvoid: Rating: 2 or 3
Reasoning framed as certainty (e.g., 'he would never gossip') rather than uncertainty

questions.title: CBT Session: Balanced Thought Phrasing
questions.scenario: Construct the final 'Balanced Thought' exactly as agreed upon by the client and therapist. It follows the format 'It may be true that... and it is also true that...'

mustInclude: It may be true that I'm scared of bothering people
It is also true that I have good friends who can and do support me
Must include the wording 'can and do support me' (not just 'can support me')
mustAvoid: Using the original hot thought as the final balanced thought (e.g., 'I shouldn't be bothering him')
Replacing the balanced thought with a different summary like 'I'm not alone' (even if consistent, it's not the phrasing agreed upon)
Paraphrasing that drops the 'can and do' refinement

questions.title: CBT Session: Emotional Shifts
questions.scenario: Compare the client's self-rated Anxiety and Fear levels (0-100%) regarding the 'Hot Thought' before the exercise versus after the thought was restructured. State the specific start and end numbers for both.

mustInclude: Anxiety: 40% before, 20% after
Fear: 60% before, 30% after
mustAvoid: Mixing up anxiety and fear values
Providing only approximate values (e.g., 'about half') instead of the stated numbers
Reporting only 'before' or only 'after' values

questions.title: CBT Session: Physical Symptoms
questions.scenario: For the homework experiment prediction, what specific physical sensations did the client identify as indicators that her anxiety is rising?

mustInclude: Knot in stomach
Shaky / shakiness
mustAvoid: Symptoms not mentioned (e.g., sweating, rapid heartbeat, headache)
Describing it as a panic attack (she explicitly contrasts it with not having a panic attack)

questions.title: CBT Session: Obstacle Management
questions.scenario: The client identified 'being too anxious to reach out' as a potential obstacle to the homework. What specific coping strategy did they agree to write down on the plan to overcome this?

mustInclude: Take a picture/photo (of the whiteboard/thought record/evidence)
Use/look at the picture as a reminder if anxiety gets high
mustAvoid: Coping strategies not agreed on in the transcript (e.g., deep breathing, calling the therapist, postponing indefinitely)
Vague answers like 'just try harder' without the concrete 'picture' strategy
